subject: Science
chapter: Exploring Substances: Acidic, Basic and Neutral

key_points: Substances are classified as acidic, basic, or neutral based on their chemical nature
Acids are sour in taste, while bases are bitter and feel soapy to the touch
Indicators are special substances used to test whether a substance is acidic or basic
The reaction between an acid and a base is called neutralization
Neutralization results in the formation of salt and water, often releasing heat
examples: Acidic substances: Lemon juice (Citric acid), Vinegar (Acetic acid), Curd (Lactic acid)
Basic substances: Baking soda, Lime water, Soap solution, Milk of magnesia
Neutral substances: Pure water, Common salt solution, Sugar solution
Natural Indicators: Turmeric, Litmus (extracted from lichens), China rose petals
formulas: Neutralization Reaction: Acid + Base → Salt + Water + Heat
Litmus Test: Acid turns Blue Litmus Red; Base turns Red Litmus Blue
Phenolphthalein: Remains colorless in Acid; turns Pink in Base
exam_tips: Always use a clean dropper when testing substances to avoid cross-contamination
Note that not all sour things are safe to taste; always use indicators for unknown chemicals
In neutralization, the salt formed can be acidic, basic, or neutral in nature
